ARGUED. Plaintiff contests the tentative ruling. Mr. Furlow presents arguments. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF RED OAK REALTY's Motion For Preliminary Injunction.

Plaintiff's motion for preliminary injunction is DENIED.

In deciding whether to issue a preliminary injunction, the court evaluates two interrelated factors: (1) the likelihood that the party seeking the injunction will ultimately prevail on the merits; and (2) the balance of harms presented, i.e., the comparative consequences of the issuance vs. non-issuance of the injunction. (Common Cause v. Board of Superiors (1989) 49 Cal.3d 432, 441-442.) These two factors balance such that "[t]he more likely it is that plaintiffs will ultimately prevail, the less severe must be the harm that they allege will occur if the injunction does not issue." (Right Site Coalition v. Los Angeles Unified School District (2008) 160 Cal.App.4th 336, 338-339.)

Here, plaintiff fails to sustain its evidentiary burden and does not show a likelihood of prevailing or that the balance of equities and harms weighs in its favor. Plaintiff's moving papers are based solely on the declaration of its owner and CEO Vanessa Bergmark. She asserts that Defendants misused Plaintiff's confidential information to solicit and improperly poach Plaintiff's agents. (Bergmark Decl., pars. 14-15.) Defendants' objection to this evidence as lacking foundation/personal knowledge is sustained; declarant fails to demonstrate a basis for her assertions. In addition, Defendants present evidence that Plaintiff's former employees-not Defendants-initiated the recruitment discussions and those discussions did not involve the alleged "confidential information." (Jenssen Decl., par. 16; Mansour Decl., pars. 23-24; Weinstein Decl., pars. 10-14, 19; Einsidler Decl., pars. 6, 11.)
There is no material evidence those employees did anything wrong in reaching out to Defendants. If Plaintiff had a proscription against its employees reaching out, it would likely constitute an illegal restraint of trade in violation of Business and Professions Code section 16600. (See Edwards v. Arthur Anderson LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, 945-947.) Plaintiff therefore fails to show a misuse of its alleged confidential information.

Order is signed.
